verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 2D top-view layout shows a flat rectangular roof plan with no slope. Page 5 and 6 3D renders depict a flat RCC roof with elevated panel structure mounted above it. Page 9-10 rooftop photos show a flat tiled surface consistent with an RCC flat roof.
verdicts.rationale: Site layout, 3D renders, and rooftop photos all consistently show a flat RCC roof.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 9-10 rooftop photos show a flat terrace with a staircase headroom structure and a yellow water tank visible on one end. The 2D plan on page 5 shows the panel array is laid out to avoid these features. The proposed PV installation area itself appears clear.
verdicts.rationale: Water tank and staircase headroom are present on the roof but the page 5 layout diagram places the panel array clear of these features; the installation area is unobstructed.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-10 show the proposed PV area on a flat rooftop with a 'PV MODULES' placard — no actual installed panels are present yet. The 3D renders on pages 5-6 depict an elevated structure but the tilt angle is not measurable from the render perspective.
verdicts.rationale: Panels are not yet installed; tilt angle cannot be verified visually from the pre-installation site photos or 3D renders.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 2D plan shows a single continuous flat roof area. Pages 5-6 3D renders confirm one roof level. Building exterior on page 6 shows a single-roof residential villa.
verdicts.rationale: Site layout and all exterior/3D photos consistently show a single roof.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.designer_notes: Initially quoted for (EcoSoch Flat Roof - Portrait Double Row) Later changed to MS elevated structure with L-clamps based on the space constraints.
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5 and 6 3D renders clearly depict solar panels raised on metal legs above the flat roof surface — an elevated MS structure. The 2D top-view on page 5 also shows the elevated arrangement.
verdicts.rationale: 3D renders on pages 5 and 6 clearly show an elevated structure lifting panels above the flat roof, consistent with the designer's answer.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 2D site layout includes a compass rose showing N/S/E/W cardinal directions. The panel array in the layout is oriented with the face pointing toward the south side of the plan. GPS coordinates (12.75°N, 77.70°E) place the site in Bangalore, where south-facing is standard for optimal yield.
verdicts.rationale: Compass on page 5 site layout is consistent with south-facing panel orientation; Bangalore location further supports south as the correct choice.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 'Staircase Details' section shows a clear interior staircase with granite steps, glass-and-metal railings, and a caption reading 'Staircase width: 1000 mm'. Page 9 also shows the staircase leading up to the terrace level.
verdicts.rationale: Dedicated staircase photo on page 8 with caption confirms staircase access to the roof.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 'Building Exterior' photo shows a fully completed 2-storey residential villa with painted plaster walls, finished windows with grilles, a paved driveway, and landscaping. No bare brick, scaffolding, or construction activity visible.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photo on page 6 shows a fully finished residential structure with no signs of ongoing construction.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 building exterior photo shows ground floor (with arched entry and parking) plus one upper floor (with balcony and grilles), plus a small staircase headroom structure visible on the roof in the 3D render on page 6. Total three levels consistent with G+1+headroom. A typical 10m height for this configuration is plausible.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior and 3D render on page 6 show G+1 floors plus a rooftop staircase headroom structure, consistent with G+1+headroom description.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 building exterior photo shows a residential villa matching the project description: residential sector, G+1+headroom, RCC flat roof, staircase access visible. Project name 'Grand Lavasa' aligns with the gated villa community visible in the photo.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photo on page 6 is consistent with all key project attributes described in the report.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 6 and 7 exterior and road photos show the surrounding area. Only standard low-voltage distribution poles are visible; no high-tension transmission towers, insulator strings, or multi-bundled HT conductors are evident near the building.
verdicts.rationale: Exterior and road photos on pages 6-7 show no HT transmission infrastructure visible in the immediate vicinity of the building.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 'Material Delivery Route' top photo shows a wide paved road directly in front of the building with an open gate/driveway. The road width appears sufficient for a delivery truck. Bottom photo on page 7 shows the side wall approach used for heavy materials (PV modules).
verdicts.rationale: Material Delivery Route photos on page 7 clearly show wide road access and unloading point in front of the building.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 shows a 1000mm-wide staircase with railings — adequate for light materials. Page 7 bottom shows heavy materials (PV modules) can be hoisted over the side wall. Page 9 shows an open terrace for staging. No low headroom, narrow bottlenecks, or blocking walls are apparent.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase (page 8) and side-wall hoisting route (page 7) provide viable paths; no blocking obstacles apparent in photos.

verdicts.question: Where will the Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery, etc. be located?
verdicts.designer_answer: At the first floor, mounted on the wall.
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 bottom photo shows two wall-mounted orange-labeled units (inverter/battery) fixed directly to a white interior wall. The equipment appears to be in an indoor room consistent with a first-floor wall mounting.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location photo on page 12 shows wall-mounted units in an interior room, consistent with first-floor wall-mount answer.

verdicts.question: Is a canopy required for protecting the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 bottom photo shows the inverter and battery mounted inside an enclosed interior room with a white painted wall — fully sheltered from weather. No outdoor exposure requiring a canopy.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment is located indoors in a sheltered room (page 12), so no canopy is needed.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Yes. The earth pit location was discussed and finalised with the customer.
verdicts.photo_observation: No dedicated earth pit location photo or marked diagram is present in the document to visually confirm that the location was finalized and shown to the customer.
verdicts.rationale: This is a process confirmation; no photo evidence of earth pit location marking or customer sign-off is available in the document.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo of the internet router or its location appears anywhere in the 14-page document.
verdicts.rationale: No router photo in the document to verify 1st floor placement.

verdicts.question: What's the Wi-Fi strength of the router near to the String inverter/Envoy?
verdicts.photo_observation: No Wi-Fi signal screenshot or measurement photograph is included in the document.
verdicts.rationale: Wi-Fi signal strength is a measurement that cannot be verified from site photos.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No WhatsApp screenshot or GPS pin confirmation photo is included in the document.
verdicts.rationale: WhatsApp location pinning is a process step that cannot be verified from site visit photos.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: Normal Three Phase
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 top photo shows the main meter panel open: a red-labeled 'MAIN METER' unit with an LCD/digital display face is visible on the left of the enclosure. Multiple coloured wires (yellow, blue, red) feed into MCBs on the right side, indicating a multi-phase supply consistent with a normal three-phase connection.
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo on page 12 shows a digital meter and multi-phase wiring consistent with a three-phase digital installation.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 top photo shows an open electrical enclosure with a digital meter labeled 'MAIN METER' and multiple MCBs with multi-coloured three-phase wiring — this is visually consistent with a three-phase digital normal meter.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photo on page 12 aligns with the declared three-phase digital meter type.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 top shows the internal layout of the main panel with MCBs and wiring visible. There is open space on the right side of the MCB bank where a solar AC termination could be connected, visible and accessible.
verdicts.rationale: Main panel photo on page 12 shows an accessible internal MCB arrangement; the termination area is visible and identifiable.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 top photo shows the main meter panel with the door open and the full interior wiring and MCBs clearly visible. The panel is not sealed; the internal components are accessible.
verdicts.rationale: Panel is shown open on page 12 with internal wiring visible, confirming the termination point is accessible and not sealed.

verdicts.question: Is a Lightning Arrestor (LA) already available at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No lightning arrestor equipment (surge arrester unit, earthing spike, or LA rod on roof) is visible in any of the 14 pages — neither in roof photos, building exterior, nor the meter panel and equipment photos.
verdicts.rationale: No LA equipment is visible anywhere in the site photos, consistent with designer's answer of No.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 top shows the main meter enclosure: the left half is fully occupied by the main digital meter and the right half is densely packed with MCBs and wiring. No visible empty DIN-rail slots or free compartment space for an additional solar meter.
verdicts.rationale: Main panel photo on page 12 shows the enclosure is fully occupied with no apparent space for a solar meter, consistent with the designer's answer.

verdicts.question: Is there space available to dig DC, AC and LA earth pits?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 11 bottom shows the side alley between the villa and the adjacent building — an open ground-level passage with visible soil/unpaved ground. Page 9 also shows open ground around the building perimeter. The side alley appears to have sufficient length for earth pit excavation.
verdicts.rationale: Side alley photo on page 11 shows open ground area alongside the building suitable for earth pit excavation.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is an LA extension provided at the far end?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5 and 6 3D renders show the elevated MS structure on the roof. No LA extension rod or spike is visible at the far end of the structure in either render.
verdicts.rationale: The 3D renders do not show an LA extension, but they are design illustrations rather than actual photos; the absence in a render does not constitute clear photographic confirmation.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is it with or without grouting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5 and 6 3D renders depict the elevated structure's base plates resting on the flat roof. Since panels are not yet installed, the actual roof surface with structure base plates and grouting is not visible in any site photo.
verdicts.rationale: Panels and structure are not yet installed; grouting cannot be confirmed from pre-installation site photos or design renders.

verdicts.question: Are there any challenges with respect to Installation and Maintenance activities?
verdicts.photo_observation: All site photos show a standard residential installation scenario: adequate staircase access (page 8), clear material delivery route (page 7), open rooftop (pages 9-10), and a straightforward cable routing path along the exterior wall (pages 10-11). No unusual constraints are apparent.
verdicts.rationale: Site photos collectively show a standard residential installation with no visually apparent unusual challenges.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 shows a well-maintained staircase with full-height glass-and-metal safety railings and 1000mm width. The flat rooftop shown on pages 9-10 has a low parapet/edge visible. No apparent hazards such as fragile skylights, unsafe edges, or steep slopes.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase on page 8 has proper safety railings; the flat roof surface shown on pages 9-10 presents no obvious safety hazard.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 9 'Material Storage' caption describes the terrace at 1st floor of the building. The photo shows an open terrace level with boundary walls — there is wall space against which panels could be stored vertically. The staircase headroom structure wall on the roof is also visible.
verdicts.rationale: Page 9 material storage area shows open terrace with boundary walls suitable for vertically leaning panel storage.

verdicts.question: Is ventilation available in the battery location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 bottom shows the equipment (inverter/battery) wall-mounted in an interior room. The photo does not show windows, vents, or ventilation grilles clearly enough to confirm adequate ventilation in the space.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location photo on page 12 does not clearly show ventilation features of the battery room.

verdicts.question: Should the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB be installed on a metal grill or wall mount?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 bottom photo shows two orange-labeled units (inverter/battery) mounted directly onto a flat white painted wall surface — no metal grill or rack is visible; it is a direct wall mount.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location photo on page 12 clearly shows direct wall mounting without any metal grill.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo with measurable wall dimensions for the solar meter cubicle placement location is present in the document. The meter panel photo on page 12 shows the existing main panel but no dimension markings or tape measure references.
verdicts.rationale: Wall dimensions for solar meter placement cannot be verified from site photos without a scale reference.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 11 bottom shows the side alley ground area. The alley appears narrow and linear; while a 3m x 1m space is plausible for a side alley, no scale reference or dimension markings are visible to confirm the exact dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit area dimensions cannot be confirmed from the site photo without a scale reference.

verdicts.question: Is LAN cable under customer scope?
verdicts.photo_observation: LAN cable scope is a contractual/commercial agreement; no photo in the document can confirm or deny this.
verdicts.rationale: Contractual responsibility for LAN cable cannot be verified from site visit photos.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9 and 10 show the proposed PV area on the rooftop. The surrounding buildings visible are of similar height (G+1), and the open sky is visible above the proposed panel zone. No tall trees, chimneys, or adjacent high-rise structures that would cast significant shadows are apparent in the photos.
verdicts.rationale: Rooftop photos on pages 9-10 show an open sky above the PV area with no significant nearby shadow-casting structures visible.
